en.extract:
The jocotoco antpitta (Grallaria ridgelyi) is an endangered species of bird in the family Grallariidae. It is found in Ecuador and Peru.


Taxonomy and systematics
The jocotoco antpitta is monotypic. Its closest relatives appear to be the chestnut-naped antpitta (G. muchalis) and the pale-billed antpitta (G. carrikeri).
The jocotoco antpitta's specific epithet honors the ornithologist Robert S. Ridgely, who took part in the initial documentation of this species in 1997. The common name refers to the local name of the bird, jocotoco, which is onomatopoetic after its hooting call.


Description
Grallaria antpittas are a "wonderful group of plump and round antbirds whose feathers are often fluffed up...they have stout bills [and] very short tails". The jocotoco antpitta is the largest member of its family. It is 20 to 24 cm (7.9 to 9.4 in) long and weighs about 150 to 200 g (5.3 to 7.1 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a black crown and brownish olive nape. They have a large, somewhat shaggy, fan-shaped white patch from the bill to below the eye. Their ear coverts are gray. Their upperparts are brownish olive with a black wash that lessens from the mantle to the rump. Their tail is reddish brown. Their flight feathers have blackish inner webs and cinnamon outer webs, and their upperwing coverts are brownish olive with a black band. Their throat is snowy white. Their underparts are mostly light gray that is darker on the flanks which also have a brownish olive wash. Their undertail coverts are brownish olive with fine black bars. Both sexes have a dark red to crimson reddish brown iris, a black bill, and blue-gray legs and feet. Juveniles are similar to adults but more muted and with a chestnut crown.


Distribution and habitat
The jocotoco antpitta is known only from a very small number of locations in southeastern Ecuador and adjacent Peru. It was originally believed to be limited to the upper Chinchipe River drainage in Zamora-Chinchipe, Ecuador, but in 2006 a population was discovered in Cordillera del Cóndor in Cajamarca, Peru. It inhabits steep slopes within wet, dense, mossy forest with Chusquea bamboo stands and silvery-leaved Cecropia trees. In Ecuador it occurs between 2,300 and 2,650 m (7,500 and 8,700 ft). The observations in Peru were made at about 2,250 m (7,400 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The jocotoco antpitta is resident throughout its range.


Feeding
The jocotoco antpitta is one of several antpittas that regularly come to feeding stations set up to view them. There they are fed earthworms, which are thought to also be a large part of their natural diet. In the wild they also feed on arthropods and other invertebrates. They are almost entirely terrestrial, seeking prey by probing leaf litter with their bill and flipping it with their bill and feet. They occasionally follow army ant swarms to capture prey disturbed by the ants.


Breeding
The jocotoco antpitta's breeding season is not known but includes November. The first nest discovered was a deep cup made mostly from decaying leaves lined with fine rootlets and fern stems. It was supported by a dense bromeliad tuft against the trunk of a tree 3.6 m (12 ft) above the ground. It contained one nestling, which both parents provisioned. The incubation period, time to fledging, and other details of parental care are not known.


Vocalization
The jocotoco antpitta's song is "a slow series of 6-10 (or more) identical notes produced at 0.5-0.6 kHz, and delivered at 1-2 second intervals". The note has been described as "a low-pitched hooting 'hoo' " and the song can last for a minute or more. It has been likened to a dog bark and a cow's moo. One call is "a staccato hoó-có-kurr" which is the inspiration for the species' common name. The species typically sings around dawn and dusk but is very responsive to recordings of its song and will answer them at any time of day. 


Status
The IUCN has assessed the jocotoco antpitta as Endangered (B1ab(iii)+2ab(iii)). It has an estimated area of occupancy of 252 km2 (97 sq mi) within an overall range of about 1,300 km2 (500 sq mi). Its estimated population of 480 to 600 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. The Tapichalaca Biological Reserve was established in 1998 by the Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco to protect the species' core range, and it also occurs at the far southern end of Podocarpus National Park. However, "[m]uch of the range is threatened by logging and gold mining, including areas within Podocarpus National Park". Its actual area of occurrence may be as small as 25 km2 (9.7 sq mi).


References


External links
BirdLife International: Jocotoco Antpitta Grallaria ridgelyi Species Factsheet.
World Land Trust: Jocotoco Antpitta Grallaria ridgelyi.

The '''jocotoco antpitta''' ('''''Grallaria ridgelyi''''') is an [[Endangered species (IUCN status)|endangered]] species of bird in the family [[Grallariidae]]. It is found in [[Ecuador]] and [[Peru]].<ref name=IUCN/><ref name=IOC14.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/antthrushes/ |title=Antthrushes, antpittas, gnateaters, tapaculos, crescentchests |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=August 2024 |access-date=19 August 2024 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The jocotoco antpitta is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC14.2/> Its closest relatives appear to be the [[chestnut-naped antpitta]] (''G. muchalis'') and the [[pale-billed antpitta]] (''G. carrikeri'').<ref name=Krabbe>{{cite journal|author1=Krabbe, Niels |author2=Agro, D.J. |author3=Rice, N.H. |author4=Jacome, M. |author5=Navarrete, L. |author6=Sornoza M., F. |year=1999|title= A new species of antpitta (Formicariidae: ''Grallaria'') from the southern Ecuadorian Andes|journal=[[Auk (journal)|Auk]]|volume=116|issue=4|pages= 882–890|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v116n04/p0882-p0890.pdf|doi=10.2307/4089669|jstor=4089669 }}</ref>

The jocotoco antpitta's [[specific epithet]] honors the [[ornithologist]] [[Robert S. Ridgely]], who took part in the initial documentation of this species in 1997. The [[common name]] refers to the local name of the bird, ''jocotoco'', which is [[onomatopoetic]] after its hooting call.<ref name=Krabbe/>

[[File:Jocotoco Antpitta.jpg|thumb|left]]

==Description==

''Grallaria'' antpittas are a "wonderful group of plump and round antbirds whose feathers are often fluffed up...they have stout bills [and] very short tails".<ref name=Ridgely2>{{cite book | last =Ridgely | first =Robert S. | last2 =Greenfield | first2 =Paul J. | title =The Birds of Ecuador: Field Guide | publisher =Cornell University Press | volume = II| date =2001 | location =Ithaca | pages =436–437, 440| isbn =978-0-8014-8721-7 }}</ref> The jocotoco antpitta is the largest member of its family. It is {{convert|20|to|24|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs about {{convert|150|to|200|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a black crown and brownish olive nape. They have a large, somewhat shaggy, fan-shaped white patch from the bill to below the eye. Their ear [[coverts]] are gray. Their upperparts are brownish olive with a black wash that lessens from the mantle to the rump. Their tail is reddish brown. Their flight feathers have blackish inner webs and cinnamon outer webs, and their upperwing coverts are brownish olive with a black band. Their throat is snowy white. Their underparts are mostly light gray that is darker on the flanks which also have a brownish olive wash. Their undertail coverts are brownish olive with fine black bars. Both sexes have a dark red to crimson reddish brown iris, a black bill, and blue-gray legs and feet. Juveniles are similar to adults but more muted and with a chestnut crown.<ref name=Krabbe/><ref name=JOAN-BOW>Greeney, H. F. (2020). Jocotoco Antpitta (''Grallaria ridgelyi''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.jocant1.01 retrieved September 5, 2024</ref><ref name=Ridgely2/><ref name=Schulenberg>Schulenberg, T.S., D.F. Stotz, D.F. Lane, J.P. O'Neill, and T.A. Parker III. 2010. ''Birds of Peru''. Revised and updated edition. Princeton University Press, Princeton, New Jersey. Plate 306</ref>{{overcite|date=October 2024}}

==Distribution and habitat==

The jocotoco antpitta is known only from a very small number of locations in southeastern Ecuador and adjacent Peru. It was originally believed to be limited to the upper [[Chinchipe River]] drainage in [[Zamora-Chinchipe]], Ecuador, but in 2006 a population was discovered in [[Cordillera del Cóndor]] in [[Cajamarca Province|Cajamarca]], Peru.<ref name=Krabbe/><ref name=ONeill>{{cite journal|author1=O'Neill, J.P.|author2=Walker, B. |author3=Claramunt, S.|author4=Alamía, L.A. |author5=Salazar, S. |author6=Mark, T. |author7=Sánchez, M.|author8=Schmitt, D.C. |author9=Schmitt, C. G. |author10=Schmitt,C.J. |author11=Takano, F. |author12=Urbay, A. |year=2021|title= An ornithological survey of the southern Cordillera del Cóndor including the first record of Jocotoco Antpitta (''Grallaria ridgelyi'') for Peru|journal= Boletín de la Unión de Ornitólogos del Perú|volume=16|issue=1|pages= 36–56|url=https://boletinunop.weebly.com/uploads/6/2/2/6/62265985/boletin_unop_vol._16_n°1_2021_-_oneill.pdf}}</ref> It inhabits steep slopes within wet, dense, mossy forest with ''[[Chusquea]]'' [[bamboo]] stands and silvery-leaved ''[[Cecropia]]'' trees. In Ecuador it occurs between {{convert|2300|and|2650|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}. The observations in Peru were made at about {{convert|2250|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Krabbe/><ref name=JOAN-BOW/><ref name=Ridgely2/><ref name=Schulenberg/><ref name=ONeill/>{{overcite|date=October 2024}}

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The jocotoco antpitta is resident throughout its range.<ref name=JOAN-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The jocotoco antpitta is one of several antpittas that regularly come to feeding stations set up to view them. There they are fed earthworms, which are thought to also be a large part of their natural diet. In the wild they also feed on [[arthropod]]s and other invertebrates. They are almost entirely terrestrial, seeking prey by probing leaf litter with their bill and flipping it with their bill and feet. They occasionally follow [[army ant]] swarms to capture prey disturbed by the ants.<ref name=Krabbe/><ref name=Ridgely2/><ref name=JOAN-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The jocotoco antpitta's breeding season is not known but includes November. The first nest discovered was a deep cup made mostly from decaying leaves lined with fine rootlets and fern stems. It was supported by a dense [[bromeliad]] tuft against the trunk of a tree {{convert|3.6|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} above the ground. It contained one nestling, which both parents provisioned. The incubation period, time to fledging, and other details of parental care are not known.<ref name=JOAN-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Grallaria-ridgelyi  |species=the jocotoco antpitta}}

===Vocalization===
The jocotoco antpitta's song is "a slow series of 6-10 (or more) identical notes produced at 0.5-0.6 kHz, and delivered at 1-2 second intervals".<ref name=JOAN-BOW/> The note has been described as "a low-pitched hooting 'hoo' " and the song can last for a minute or more.<ref name=Ridgely2/> It has been likened to a dog bark and a cow's moo.<ref name=JOAN-BOW/> One call is "a staccato ''hoó-có-kurr''" which is the inspiration for the species' common name.<ref name=Krabbe/><ref name=JOAN-BOW/> The species typically sings around dawn and dusk but is very responsive to recordings of its song and will answer them at any time of day.<ref name=Ridgely2/><ref name=JOAN-BOW/> 

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the jocotoco antpitta as Endangered (B1ab(iii)+2ab(iii)). It has an estimated area of occupancy of {{convert|252|km2|sqmi|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} within an overall range of about {{convert|1300|km2|sqmi|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. Its estimated population of 480 to 600 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. The [[Tapichalaca Biological Reserve]] was established in 1998 by the [[Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco]] to protect the species' core range, and it also occurs at the far southern end of [[Podocarpus National Park]]. However, "[m]uch of the range is threatened by logging and gold mining, including areas within Podocarpus National Park". Its actual area of occurrence may be as small as {{convert|25|km2|sqmi|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Freile>{{cite journal |last1=Freile |first1=Juan F. |last2=Luis Parra |first2=Juan |last3=Graham |first3=Catherine H. |title=Distribution and conservation of ''Grallaria'' and ''Grallaricula'' antpittas (Grallariidae) in Ecuador |journal=Bird Conservation International |date=December 2010 |volume=20 |issue=4 |pages=410–431 |doi=10.1017/S0959270910000262}}</ref>
